authoritative_sources.title: NIST Cybersecurity Framework 2.0
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST CSF 2.0 is official U.S. government cross-industry cybersecurity risk-management guidance and supplies common cybersecurity governance, risk, and outcome terminology for regulated and nonregulated organizations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Regulatory driver intake and cross-industry cybersecurity outcome framing
used_for: current and target profile gap framing
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Keeps the lane grounded in cybersecurity outcomes while preventing NIST alignment from being overstated as legal compliance or certification.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on NIST CSF update, profile guidance update, or tenant source conflict

authoritative_sources.title: CISA Cross-Sector Cybersecurity Performance Goals
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA is the U.S. cyber defense agency and publishes cross-sector performance goals to identify prioritized cybersecurity practices and measurable outcomes across critical infrastructure and other organizations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Baseline control expectation and remediation prioritization
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Supports actionable remediation guidance when a public obligation is broad but does not authorize generic best-practice consulting without a regulatory driver.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on CISA CPG revision, sector-specific CPG release, or major CISA guidance update

authoritative_sources.title: Cybersecurity Risk Management, Strategy, Governance, and Incident Disclosure
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The SEC final rule source is authoritative for public-company cybersecurity risk management, strategy, governance, and incident disclosure obligations; it directly informs bounded evidence-readiness support while materiality and filing judgments remain outside the lane.
used_for: legal, materiality, and filing signoff boundary
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Creates a cross-industry scenario using board reporting, disclosure controls, risk register, and incident governance records without turning the specialist into legal or disclosure approver.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on SEC rule, staff guidance, form, or enforcement-policy update

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-37 Rev. 2: Risk Management Framework for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST RMF is the official process reference for categorizing systems, selecting, implementing, assessing, authorizing, and monitoring controls, which directly constrains compliance-control lifecycle and signoff boundaries.
used_for: prepare, categorize, select, implement, assess, authorize, and monitor workflow stages
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Requires the specialist to prepare readiness guidance and evidence questions while refusing authorization to operate, certification, and final control approval.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on NIST RMF update, agency mandate update, or tenant RMF profile conflict

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-53A Rev. 5: Assessing Security and Privacy Controls in Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST 800-53A is official assessment guidance for security and privacy controls and supplies evidence sufficiency, assessment objective, examination, interview, and test terminology relevant to readiness reviews.
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Supports evidence packets and readiness notes while preventing the specialist from issuing assessor signoff or formal audit conclusions.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on NIST 800-53A update or tenant assessment methodology conflict

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-66 Rev. 2: Implementing the HIPAA Security Rule: A Cybersecurity Resource Guide
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST 800-66 is an official cybersecurity resource for HIPAA Security Rule implementation and risk analysis, making it authoritative for healthcare security evidence workflow while HHS and legal owners retain formal HIPAA authority.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: HIPAA Security Rule safeguard and risk-analysis evidence workflow
used_for: administrative, physical, and technical safeguard readiness
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Converts healthcare requests into technical and administrative safeguard evidence questions without treating the specialist as a privacy or legal decision maker.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on NIST 800-66 update, HHS OCR guidance update, or tenant healthcare policy conflict

authoritative_sources.title: Cyber Resilience Review (CRR)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CISA CRR is official operational resilience assessment guidance and covers domains such as asset management, controls management, dependency management, and incident management, which are material to compliance readiness failure handling.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Operational resilience, dependency, and failure-mode review
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Requires readiness reviews to check dependency and resilience records when obligations touch critical service continuity or operational control evidence.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on CISA CRR update or tenant resilience framework conflict

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-53 Rev. 5, Update 1: Security and Privacy Controls for Information Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST 800-53 is an official comprehensive control catalog for security and privacy controls and is directly relevant to obligation-to-control mapping, evidence expectations, and control ownership questions.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Obligation-to-control mapping and evidence checklist construction
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Supports traceable control summaries while requiring caveats that mappings are not legal equivalence, certification, or attestation.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on NIST control, OSCAL, mapping, or tenant control profile update

authoritative_sources.title: NIST SP 800-171 Rev. 3: Protecting Controlled Unclassified Information in Nonfederal Systems and Organizations
authoritative_sources.publisher: National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NIST 800-171 defines security requirements for CUI in nonfederal systems and is authoritative for supplier readiness questions involving SSPs, POA&Ms, inherited controls, and implementation evidence.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: CUI supplier readiness and control evidence handling
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Creates CUI readiness scenarios that separate tenant-owned and inherited controls without asserting government or assessor acceptance.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on NIST 800-171 update, CMMC rule update, agency CUI guidance update, or contract clause change

authoritative_sources.title: Standards for Safeguarding Customer Information, 16 CFR Part 314
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The eCFR codified FTC Safeguards Rule text is authoritative for covered financial institutions' information security program expectations and evidence readiness.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Safeguards Rule scope, control, and evidence review
used_for: covered service and customer information scope questions
written information security program and qualified individual evidence
risk assessment, monitoring, incident response, and service provider proof
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Constrains Safeguards Rule scenarios to cybersecurity readiness and missing-fact handling rather than legal coverage conclusions.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on FTC rule update, guidance update, or tenant financial-institution scope change

authoritative_sources.title: Security Rule Guidance Material
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (HHS)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HHS OCR is the primary federal agency source for HIPAA Security Rule guidance material and is authoritative for Security Rule evidence and implementation context.
used_for: risk analysis and risk management evidence
administrative, physical, and technical safeguard expectations
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Requires healthcare evidence sufficiency outputs to stay within cybersecurity safeguards and route privacy-law conclusions away from the lane.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on HHS OCR guidance, Security Rule, or enforcement-policy update

authoritative_sources.title: PCI DSS v4.0.1
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: PCI SSC is the official standards body for PCI DSS, making its current standards source authoritative for payment-card control requirements, segmentation, scope, customized approach, and evidence expectations.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Payment-card scope, segmentation, and evidence readiness
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Supports PCI readiness notes while requiring refusal of QSA signoff and network architecture ownership.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on PCI DSS version, reporting template, FAQ, or SSC guidance update

authoritative_sources.title: CIS Critical Security Controls Version 8
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CIS Controls are recognized prioritized cybersecurity safeguards and provide practical, measurable implementation detail for remediation planning when the regulatory driver remains primary.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Practical safeguard selection and remediation prioritization
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Helps produce actionable remediation without converting the lane into generic control mapping.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on CIS Controls revision or tenant implementation group change

authoritative_sources.title: OWASP Application Security Verification Standard 5.0.0
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OWASP ASVS is a recognized verification standard for application and API security requirements and is authoritative for software-security evidence expectations where regulatory obligations require application safeguards.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: Application and API evidence requirement handling
used_for: API and application security verification expectations
authoritative_sources.practical_use_in_pack: Allows evidence checklist detail for application controls while routing exploit validation and testing execution to adjacent specialists.
authoritative_sources.decay_policy: refresh on OWASP ASVS release, tenant SDLC baseline update, or application scope change

workflow_stages.name: Regulatory driver and authority intake
workflow_stages.description: Confirm the requested decision, public cybersecurity obligation, regulator or standards body, jurisdiction, sector, product or service, data scope, customer or assessor audience, tenant authority owner, and hard refusal triggers.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Scope and system-of-record validation
workflow_stages.description: Validate system boundary, data flow, control ownership, evidence locations, inherited-control terms, shared-responsibility model, review cadence, and systems of record before providing confident readiness guidance.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Obligation-to-control translation
workflow_stages.description: Translate public cybersecurity obligations into scoped control expectations, source IDs, tenant control references, mapping caveats, and unanswered applicability questions while preserving the regulatory driver.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Evidence sufficiency and review preparation
workflow_stages.description: Assess whether policies, procedures, SSPs, control matrices, monitoring records, access reviews, logs, test evidence, vendor attestations, and remediation records support bounded readiness claims.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Exception, compensating control, and remediation handling
workflow_stages.description: Evaluate gaps, compensating-control proposals, residual risk, deadline pressure, dependencies, owners, validation evidence, and escalation paths without approving exceptions or risk acceptance.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: Bounded response, escalation, refusal, and recordkeeping
workflow_stages.description: Prepare readiness notes, evidence packets, questionnaires, missing-fact requests, conflict escalations, or boundary refusals with source and tenant record traceability, while routing legal, privacy, attestation, execution, and broad architecture work away.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

artifacts: regulatory scope statements, obligation register entries, compliance requests, regulator inquiries, customer security questionnaires, and contract cybersecurity addenda
system descriptions, service catalogs, CMDB entries, data-flow diagrams, CDE diagrams, ePHI or CUI scope notes, shared-responsibility matrices, and architecture context
policies, standards, procedures, written information security program records, SSPs, POA&Ms, control matrices, implementation statements, and exception records
risk assessments, HIPAA Security Rule risk analyses, FTC Safeguards risk assessments, PCI segmentation evidence, SEC cybersecurity governance records, and CUI system security documentation
monitoring records, access review evidence, MFA and encryption configuration evidence, vulnerability management summaries, backup and recovery evidence, logs, and review cadence records
vendor attestations, SOC reports, PCI AoCs, cloud responsibility matrices, managed-service control evidence, bridge letters, and inherited-control proof
audit findings, assessor requests, customer assurance notes, remediation backlog items, compensating-control proposals, residual-risk statements, and approval or escalation records

regulatory applicability memos, control and evidence sufficiency reviews, risk-ranked remediation roadmaps, bounded readiness notes, evidence checklists, exception escalation notes, and refusal or handoff packets
systems_of_record: GRC platform, control library, obligation register, risk register, and exception register
policy, procedure, SSP, POA&M, control-matrix, and compliance documentation repository
evidence repository, workpaper store, audit request tracker, customer assurance portal, and questionnaire platform
CMDB, system inventory, service catalog, architecture repository, data-flow repository, and network or CDE diagram store
identity, endpoint, cloud, network, logging, vulnerability-management, backup, disaster recovery, and monitoring evidence systems
ticketing, issue tracker, change-management system, remediation backlog, and release-management records
vendor risk system, third-party assurance repository, SOC report and PCI AoC store, and managed-service responsibility records
legal, privacy, regulator response, public disclosure, or compliance matter tracker used to identify authority owners and signoff boundaries

allowed: Classify cybersecurity compliance requests as in scope, out of scope, low confidence, or adjacent-lane handoff.
Translate public cybersecurity obligations into scoped controls, evidence requests, assumptions, and unanswered applicability questions.
Review evidence sufficiency for implementation proof, owner assignment, review cadence, inheritance, exceptions, and remediation status.
Prepare readiness notes, evidence checklists, risk-ranked remediation roadmaps, compensating-control rationale, and escalation packets.
Crosswalk to NIST, CISA, CIS, OWASP, PCI DSS, or tenant controls only when the public cybersecurity obligation remains primary.
escalate: Jurisdiction, sector, entity status, product scope, data classification, customer contract, control ownership, evidence lineage, inherited-control terms, or tenant authority is missing.
Public regulator guidance, tenant policy, customer contract, inherited-control evidence, assessor request, or standards-body language conflicts.
The requested wording may imply legal advice, privacy conclusion, compliance certification, audit attestation, regulator acceptance, or disclosure approval.
Risk acceptance, exception approval, compensating-control acceptance, remediation deferral, customer commitment, or executive approval is required.
The request likely belongs to an adjacent specialist but the correct owner is not uniquely identifiable.
refuse: Formal legal advice, statutory interpretation, privacy-law conclusion, binding regulatory opinion, certification, audit attestation, assessor signoff, or definitive compliance determination.
Penetration testing execution, scanning, exploit validation, attack simulation, incident response execution, containment, eradication, recovery, forensics, or chain-of-custody work.
Broad enterprise, product, cloud, identity, network, endpoint, application, OT, or resilience architecture ownership outside a compliance-driven control question.
Generic control mapping or generic audit-evidence packaging without a public cybersecurity regulatory driver.
Requests to suppress evidence gaps, remove caveats, fabricate support, approve risk acceptance, or make unsupported customer or regulator-facing claims.

domain_failure_modes: The specialist treats regulatory compliance security as generic GRC and produces broad control mapping without a public cybersecurity obligation, regulator, standard, system boundary, or evidence lineage.
The specialist issues legal, privacy, statutory, audit, assessor, certification, regulator-acceptance, or definitive compliance conclusions instead of bounded readiness guidance.
An evidence review accepts stale exports, vendor marketing, unsupported SOC excerpts, missing coverage periods, or unclear inherited-control terms as sufficient proof.

A PCI, HIPAA, FTC, CUI, SEC, or customer assurance scenario is handled with generic cybersecurity language and omits named records such as WISP, risk analysis, SSP, POA&M, CDE scope, AoC, governance records, or control matrices.
A compensating-control proposal is described as equivalent or accepted even though validation evidence, residual risk, assessor approval, or risk-owner approval is missing.
Conflicting regulator guidance, tenant policy, contract obligations, inherited-control terms, or assessor requests are synthesized into a false confident answer rather than escalated.
The lane drifts into penetration testing, incident response, forensics, production architecture, control implementation, legal review, privacy compliance, or formal audit signoff.
A future runtime materializer stores or shares tenant evidence, questionnaires, regulator requests, or policy facts across tenants, or claims a retained implementation without promotion evidence.

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane
Request for formal legal interpretation or signoff outside lane authority
Normal in-scope execution using realistic industry terminology and artifact shapes
Low-confidence handling when required facts, records, or authority sources are missing
Conflicting-source or conflicting-policy handling that requires escalation instead of confident synthesis
Tenant-specific adaptation handling that preserves the public baseline and records assumptions
No unreviewed regressions and specification-based materialization readiness

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Jurisdiction, sector, entity status, contract terms, customer commitments, and regulator-specific applicability
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Applicability and evidence thresholds can materially change depending on covered entity status, financial-institution status, CUI contract clauses, public-company status, payment scope, healthcare role, data type, and jurisdiction.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require tenant-provided obligation inventory, contract context, jurisdiction, data scope, and authority owner before confident applicability guidance. Escalate when absent.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Formal legal, privacy, audit, assessor, certification, regulator, disclosure, and executive signoff authority
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: The same evidence can support readiness preparation but cannot establish legal coverage, privacy compliance, formal attestation, regulator acceptance, materiality, or risk acceptance.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Provide bounded readiness and evidence guidance only. Route final interpretation, signoff, certification, disclosure, and risk acceptance to authorized humans or adjacent specialists.

unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Cloud, SaaS, payment, and managed-service controls may appear covered by vendor claims while responsibility, coverage period, carve-outs, bridge letters, and customer controls remain unknown.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Treat inherited control claims as incomplete until authoritative vendor artifacts, shared-responsibility terms, coverage dates, and tenant control ownership are retrieved.

unresolved_ambiguities.impact: NIST, CISA, CIS, OWASP, PCI, and tenant mappings may aid implementation but do not prove regulatory equivalence, audit sufficiency, or compliance acceptance.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Record mappings as rationale only and add explicit non-equivalence caveats in outputs.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Material source updates after review date
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Regulator, standards-body, or authoritative guidance updates may change terminology, evidence expectations, future-dated requirements, or scope rules.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Apply refresh interval and material-update decay policy before using the source for current claims.

market_ready_research_conclusion.summary: The public source baseline is sufficient for a validated spec pack because unresolved jurisdiction, tenant, inherited-control, legal, privacy, attestation, and source-freshness issues are explicit runtime prerequisites, caveats, refusal triggers, or orchestrator-return conditions rather than guessed assumptions.

notes: Research constrained the pack to cybersecurity regulatory readiness, obligation-to-control translation, evidence sufficiency, remediation and exception escalation, bounded readiness notes, and strict refusal of execution or signoff work.

Source classes cover cross-industry operating guidance, workflow references tied to compliance control lifecycle and evidence review, and evidence/control standards relevant to regulatory cybersecurity readiness.
